Issues in Special Education

Diagnoses & Designations

A study tutorial for Day 4 Part 1. Use it to review the difference between a diagnosis and a school designation, Special Education category codes, K–12 funding, the 1701 list, required documentation, and case manager responsibilities.

Diagnosis ≠ Designation IEP required for designation 1701 list Funding is district-level Case manager

1. Big Ideas to Remember

Core

A diagnosis is given by a professional.

Examples include Autism, diabetes, learning disability, and selective mutism.

School

A designation is given by the school district.

It is submitted to the Ministry of Education for funding and support planning.

Important

A diagnosis does not automatically mean a designation.

The district must review the educational impact and the level of support required.

Quick memory trick: Diagnosis = medical/professional identification. Designation = school district category for educational support and Ministry reporting.

2. Diagnosis vs Designation

Diagnosis

  • Given by a qualified professional, such as a doctor, psychologist, or speech-language pathologist.
  • Names a condition or learning need.
  • Examples: Autism, diabetes, learning disability, selective mutism.

Designation

  • Given by the school district.
  • Submitted to the Ministry of Education for funding.
  • Requires review of diagnosis or other evidence, educational impact, and required support.
  • Students with designations must have a current IEP for the school year.
EA point: A designation supports planning and services, but it does not mean the EA is “attached” to one student all day.

3. Special Education Category Codes

The quiz may ask for the letter code for a particular designation. This table summarizes the codes shown in the slideshow.

TypeLevel / FundingCodeDesignation Category
Low incidenceLevel 1APhysically Dependent — Multiple Needs
BDeaf-Blind
Level 2CModerate to Profound Intellectual Disabilities
DPhysical Disability or Chronic Health Impairment
EVisual Impairment
FDeaf or Hard of Hearing
GAutism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
Level 3HStudents Requiring Intensive Behaviour Intervention or Students with Serious Mental Illness
High incidenceBasic allocation onlyKMild Intellectual Disabilities
PGifted
QLearning Disabilities
RStudents Requiring Behaviour Support or Students with Mental Illness
Low incidence means a relatively low percentage of students and generally higher support needs. High incidence means more common categories with less intensive support than low incidence categories.

4. Steps to a Designation

If there is a previous diagnosis
  1. Parents share diagnosis reports with the school.
  2. The school district reviews whether the student meets the criteria for the corresponding designation.
  3. If the student meets the requirements, the district gives the designation.
  4. The student can be included on the 1701 list submitted to the Ministry for funding.
If there is no previous diagnosis
  1. Parents or school staff raise concerns.
  2. The doctor or school may refer the child to a specialist.
  3. The child is assessed and may receive a diagnosis.
  4. Reports are given to the school for review.
  5. The district checks designation criteria and assigns a designation if requirements are met.
  6. The school can report the student on the 1701 list for Ministry funding.
Variation: Some assessments happen through the school, such as psycho-educational testing. Families may also pursue private testing. The exact pathway depends on the student and category.

5. K–12 Funding and Special Needs Funding

Basic allocation

The basic allocation is a standard amount per school-age student enrolled in a district. The slide lists the 2026/27 amount as $9,015.

The basic allocation includes support for high-incidence categories such as learning disabilities, mild intellectual disabilities, moderate behaviour supports, and gifted students.

Special needs supplementary funding

Students with special needs may require additional support and accommodations to access and participate in education.

Additional support may include staff, specialized learning materials, physical accommodations or equipment, and assessments.

Very important: Special needs funding is given to boards of education / districts. It is not targeted to specific students. In Burnaby, EAs are assigned to schools, not tied to one student.

6. 1701, Documentation, Case Manager, and Maintenance

What is a 1701?

  • A list of students with a current designation.
  • Updated by districts twice a year: October and February.
  • Submitted to the Ministry of Education for Special Education funding.
  • May be audited, so files must be complete and up to date.

What must documentation include?

  • Assessment and identification related to the category criteria.
  • Current IEP with appropriate goals.
  • Support services related to the student’s needs.
  • Evidence parent was offered opportunity to consult on the IEP.
  • Evidence of learning activities in accordance with the IEP.
  • Methods for measuring the student’s progress.

Case manager responsibilities

  • Ensures documentation is up to date.
  • Distributes information to appropriate team members.
  • Communicates with team members.
  • Sets up meetings, such as IEP meetings.

Maintenance of designation

A student may lose a designation if they no longer require support to access the curriculum.

Example: a student with diabetes may need more support in primary grades but later learn to self-manage.

7. Practice: Billy Scenario

Billy is a Grade 4 student with Autism. You support the class every morning from 9:00–11:30. Use the questions below to practise for discussion or quiz review.

1. Billy was diagnosed with Autism. Who would have given this diagnosis?

A qualified professional, such as a doctor, psychologist, or other qualified practitioner following the appropriate standards for diagnosis.

2. Billy is given a G designation. Who gave him this designation?

The school district gives the designation after reviewing whether Billy meets the Ministry criteria for that category.

3. What does G mean?

G means Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD).

4. What does the designation entitle Billy to?

It supports access to educational planning, a current IEP, and appropriate educational supports. It does not automatically mean a specific EA schedule or one-to-one support all day.

5. Why is “Billy is entitled to direct 1:1 EA support from 9–3 because he has autism” false?

Because a diagnosis or designation does not automatically equal full-day one-to-one EA support. Funding is provided to the district, not directly to Billy, and EAs are assigned based on school and student support needs.

6. Name two things that must be in Billy’s file.

Examples: assessment/identification evidence, current IEP, support services outlined in the IEP, evidence parents were offered consultation, learning activities linked to the IEP, and methods for measuring progress.

8. Quick Review Questions

What is the difference between diagnosis and designation?

A diagnosis is given by a professional and identifies a condition or learning need. A designation is given by the school district and submitted to the Ministry for funding and planning.

Does a diagnosis automatically mean a designation?

No. The district must consider educational impact and the level of support required.

Do all students with designations need an IEP?

Yes. Students with designations require a current IEP for the school year to maintain the designation.

What is the 1701?

It is a list of students with current designations, updated twice a year and submitted to the Ministry of Education for Special Education funding.

Is special needs funding targeted to one specific student?

No. The funds are provided to boards of education to support the needs of students within the district.

What is one responsibility of a case manager?

Ensuring documentation is up to date, communicating with team members, distributing information to appropriate team members, or setting up meetings such as IEP meetings.

9. Key PPT Slide Gallery

These selected slide images are included so you can review the original class material while studying the explanations above.

10. One-Paragraph Summary

In special education, a diagnosis is given by a qualified professional, while a designation is given by the school district and submitted to the Ministry of Education for funding. A diagnosis does not automatically mean a student will receive a designation; the school district must consider the student’s educational needs and required supports. Students with designations need a current IEP, and their documentation must show assessment evidence, goals, services, parent consultation opportunity, learning activities, and ways to measure progress. The 1701 is the list of students with current designations submitted for funding, but the funding goes to the district and is not targeted directly to one student. For EAs, the key point is to understand the student’s support needs while remembering that support is planned by the school team, documented through the IEP, and coordinated by the case manager.
